features.popup:
Little Mountain Pictograph Site (067-0107)

One of only 2 pictograph sites in Virginia · NRHP 1991 · Red ochre handprint & sun glyph

Location: Near Blackstone, Nottoway County, Virginia (address restricted — approximate)

One of only two known pictograph sites in Virginia. Three red ochre (hematite) pictographs in a small sandstone rockshelter (<15 sq meters, ceiling ~3m at mouth): (1) a left human handprint, (2) a possible 'sun glyph' or overlapping handprints, and (3) a 'turkey foot' motif ~9 ft above grade. Good to excellent condition at time of 1990 survey.

Hranicky (1990) argued the shelter's orientation causes sunlight to illuminate the painted wall only around winter solstice — making this a potential prehistoric astronomical observatory. Historical graffiti (1887, 1897, 1903) was carefully placed to avoid the pictographs, suggesting local awareness of their significance. Archaeological testing found no prehistoric artifacts. In Nottoway (Cheroenhaka) territory — Iroquoian-speaking people whose homeland centered on the Nottoway River.

Sources: NRHP nomination (Opperman 1990/1991); Hranicky 1990; Swauger 1984; Faulkner 1986; Virginia DHR 067-0107

Browns Island Petroglyph (46HK8)

DESTROYED / SUBMERGED · First WV archaeological site ever documented (1785)

Location: Hancock County, WV — Ohio River island opposite Weirton

The first archaeological site ever documented in West Virginia, recorded by Benjamin Barton ca. 1785 (published 1799). 47 designs recorded by Swauger on flat sandstone at the water's edge of this Ohio River island: turtle, bear paw, bird, snake motifs. Permanently inundated by the Ohio River dam system. The loss of Browns Island represents the beginning of a pattern of industrial destruction of West Virginia's rock art heritage that continued for two centuries.

Sources: Barton 1799; Swauger 1974 (Rock Art of the Upper Ohio Valley); Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Half Moon Site (46HK29)

DESTROYED / SUBMERGED · Documented July 4, 1838 by James McBride

Location: Hancock County, WV — Ohio River bank ~1 mile below Browns Island

Documented by James McBride on July 4, 1838 (original drawings survive). Turtle, bear paw, rabbit tracks, bird tracks, human figures. Either destroyed or permanently submerged by the Ohio River dam system.

Sources: McBride 1838; Swauger 1974; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Table Rock & Timmons Farm Petroglyphs (46OH36, 46OH64)

Wheeling area · Upper Ohio Valley petroglyph tradition

Location: Ohio County, WV — near Wheeling (approximate)

Two petroglyph sites near Wheeling surveyed by James Swauger (1960, published 1961). Part of the Upper Ohio Valley petroglyph tradition characterized by Algonquian motifs including thunderbirds, underwater panthers, and serpents.

Sources: Swauger 1960/1961, 1974; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Clifton Heights & Elm Grove Petroglyphs (relocated)

RELOCATED · Now at Grave Creek Mound Archaeological Complex, Moundsville

Location: Grave Creek Mound Archaeological Complex, 801 Jefferson Ave, Moundsville, WV

Two petroglyphs relocated from Ohio County to the Grave Creek Mound Archaeological Complex. The Clifton Heights stone was originally from a hillside ~8 miles north of Wheeling. The Elm Grove stone was reported in 2019. Both are now on display at the museum alongside the famous Grave Creek Mound — the largest Adena burial mound in North America (69 ft tall, 295 ft diameter, built ca. 250–150 BCE).

Sources: Swauger 1974; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Hamilton Farm / Evansville Pike Petroglyphs

Massive cliff panel: 150 ft × 50 ft · ~50 species of animals · Full-length female figure

Location: Monongalia County, WV — ~4 miles from Morgantown on Evansville pike (approximate)

Massive cliff panel of white sandstone, 150 ft long by 50 ft wide, with ~50 species of animals, birds, reptiles, and fish, plus a full-length female figure and numerous animal footprints. Described by Mallery (1893) as the 'Evansville pike pictured rocks.' One of the most extensive petroglyph panels in the state.

Sources: Mallery 1893; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Indian Cave Petroglyphs & Pictographs (46HN1)

NRHP 1976 · One of only 2 WV pictograph sites · Occupation AD 500–1675

Location: Near Good Hope, Harrison County, WV (approximate)

Rockshelter with 20 ft × 4 ft petroglyph panel on back wall, PLUS pictographs — making this one of only two recorded pictograph sites in West Virginia (the wet climate destroyed most painted surfaces). First reported by W.H. Holmes (Bureau of American Ethnology) in 1889. Holmes found hammerstones and worked hematite at the site.

Pottery indicates occupation spanning AD 500–1675. Studied by Swauger in 1970; petroglyphs described as 'virtually unchanged' in the NRHP nomination. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1976.

Sources: Holmes 1889; Swauger 1970, 1974; NRHP 1976; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Berlin Cave Pictograph/Petroglyph

Possibly one of only 2 WV pictograph sites · Reported by McWhorter to Mallery (1893)

Location: Near Berlin, Lewis County, WV (approximate)

Cave with pictographs reported by L.Y. McWhorter to Mallery (1893). Minimal description survives: 'pictographs in a cave.' If confirmed as a pictograph site, this would be one of only two in West Virginia. Status unknown.

Sources: Mallery 1893; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Mount Pleasant Petroglyphs

Totemic characters at foot of hills · North side of Kanawha River

Location: ~2 miles above Mount Pleasant, Mason County, WV (approximate)

Totemic characters at the foot of hills flanking the Kanawha River, north side. No detailed modern documentation. Part of the broader pattern of petroglyph placement at river crossings and ford sites throughout the Kanawha Valley.

Sources: Mallery 1893; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Mount Carbon Petroglyphs

Cliffs near mouth of Kanawha · Near the famous Mount Carbon Stone Walls

Location: Opposite Mount Carbon, Fayette County border, WV (approximate)

Petroglyphs cut into sandstone cliffs near the mouth of the Kanawha River. Located near the famous Mount Carbon Stone Walls — prehistoric stone fortifications of debated origin. The proximity of petroglyphs to defensive stoneworks suggests this was a significant cultural landscape node.

Sources: Mallery 1893; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Charleston Area Petroglyphs

DESTROYED · Rock broken up for building construction

Location: ~6 miles above Charleston, Kanawha County, WV (approximate)

Rock near the water's edge with bear outline, turkey tracks, and other markings. Tradition held this was a canoe/boat landing on a north-south trail. 'The rock had been broken and used in the construction of buildings' — destroyed for building stone. A common fate for Kanawha Valley petroglyphs.

Sources: Mallery 1893; Norris (Bureau of Ethnology)

Little Coal River Pictographs

One of only 2 WV pictograph sites · Red pigment · 'Similar to Tazewell County, Virginia'

Location: Boone County, WV — 6–8 miles above Coal River confluence (approximate)

Animals (deer, panther?), circles in dark red pigment on a low sandstone escarpment facing Little Coal River. Mallery noted these were 'similar in general appearance to those in Tazewell county, Virginia' — connecting the Little Coal River pictographs to the Paint Lick Mountain tradition across the state line. Already 'rather faint from disintegration of the surface' in 1893.

Sources: Mallery 1893; Norris (Bureau of Ethnology); Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Beards Fork Petroglyphs

Sandstone shelf under overhang · Animal representations · Near Mount Carbon Stone Walls

Location: Fayette County, WV (38.064°N, 81.226°W — precise, WV Explorer)

Petroglyphs on sandstone shelf beneath an overhang along the left fork of Beards Fork of Loop Creek (Kanawha tributary). Also called 'Chisled Rocks' or 'Lettered Rocks.' Animal representations. Located near the Mount Carbon Stone Walls. Property of Pocahontas Surface International.

Sources: Maslowski 2024 (e-WV); WV Explorer

Salt Rock Petroglyphs

Fort Ancient · 6-foot anthropomorph with 'weeping eye' mask · ca. AD 1550–1650

Location: Cabell County, WV — near Guyandotte River (~38.345°N, 82.240°W)

Two large sandstone boulders ~100 yards from the northeast bank of the Guyandotte River. The dominant image is a 6-foot anthropomorph with a 'weeping eye' mask — a hallmark of the Fort Ancient culture (ca. AD 1550–1650) and the broader Southeastern Ceremonial Complex. Also includes a bird figure and a hybrid deer-serpent creature.

First documented by Squier & Davis in 1846 (published 1848 in Ancient Monuments of the Mississippi Valley — the first publication of the Smithsonian Institution). Connected to the Gue Farm Fort Ancient village site nearby. On private property, public access prohibited.

Sources: Squier & Davis 1848; Swauger 1974; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Luther Elkins / Lynco / Horse Creek Petroglyphs

Clusters, sunburst, swastika-like pattern, turkey tracks · ca. AD 1300–1500

Location: Wyoming County, WV (37.682°N, 81.662°W — from historical marker)

Low cliff near the foot of a hill overlooking upper Clear Fork of the Guyandotte River. Clusters of vertical linear and curvilinear designs, sunburst pattern, swastika-like pattern, and turkey tracks. Recorded 1964 by Oscar Mairs and Hillis Youse. Professional study in 1970 concluded: late prehistory, ca. 1300–1500 AD, by American Aborigines. Some (debunked) claims of Ogham/Irish origin have been rejected by archaeologists.

Sources: Mairs & Youse 1964; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV); WV Historical Marker

Dingess Petroglyphs

RELOCATED · Found 1987 at strip mine · Now at Laurel Lake WMA

Location: Laurel Lake Wildlife Management Area, Lenore, Mingo County, WV (display location)

Five panels of abstract markings found in 1987 under a rocky outcrop at a strip mine near Dingess. Transported to Laurel Lake Wildlife Management Area at Lenore for preservation. Investigated by Robert L. Pyle (Archaeological Archives, Inc.). The discovery at a strip mine site underscores how coal mining has both destroyed and occasionally revealed West Virginia's archaeological record.

Sources: Pyle; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Panther Petroglyph / Water-Panther Stone

RELOCATED · Underwater Panther motif · Now at Tu-Endie-Wei State Park

Location: Tu-Endie-Wei State Park, Point Pleasant, Mason County, WV (display location)

Originally from Leon, WV, ~13 miles upstream on the Kanawha River. Depicts the Water Panther / Underwater Serpent — one of the most powerful beings in Algonquian cosmology, master of the underworld waters. The slab was once used as a staircase by a settler before its significance was recognized. Now housed in a miniature log cabin for protection at Tu-Endie-Wei State Park ('Point Pleasant' in Shawnee).

Sources: Swauger 1974; Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Ceredo Petroglyph

Wayne County · Minimal documentation

Location: Near Ceredo, Wayne County, WV (approximate)

Petroglyph site near Ceredo. No detailed public description available. Part of the broader distribution of rock art sites along the Guyandotte and Ohio River drainages in southwestern West Virginia.

Sources: Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)

Wildcat Branch Petroglyph

Wayne County · Minimal documentation

Location: Wayne County, WV (approximate)

Petroglyph site in Wayne County. No detailed public description available.

Sources: Maslowski 2024 (e-WV)
